---
title: "Two-Stage Smooth-then-Differentiate"
author: "Simon Frost"
date: today
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
  gfm:
    toc: true
engine: julia
julia:
  exeflags: ["--project=.."]
---

## Overview

The `TwoStageSolver` implements the simplest baseline approach for partially specified models (Wood 2001, Macdonald & Husmeier 2015):

1. **Stage 1 — Smooth**: Fit cubic splines to each observed state independently → $\hat{y}_k(t)$ and $d\hat{y}_k/dt$
2. **Stage 2 — Match**: Optimize unknown function parameters by minimizing the mismatch between smoothed derivatives and the ODE right-hand side

This avoids ODE integration entirely (like `BNGSolver`), making it fast and robust. The key difference from BNG is its simplicity — no Bayesian interpretation, just penalized least squares matching.

```{julia}
using PartiallySpecifiedModels
using OrdinaryDiffEq
using Plots
using Random
Random.seed!(123)
```

## SIR Model with Unknown Force of Infection

We model an epidemic where the force of infection $\beta(I)$ is unknown:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta(I) \cdot S, \qquad \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta(I) \cdot S - \gamma I$$

The true function is frequency-dependent: $\beta(I) = 0.3 I$.

```{julia}
β_true(I) = 0.3 * I

function sir!(du, u, p, t)
    S, I = u
    du[1] = -p.β(I) * S
    du[2] = p.β(I) * S - 0.1 * I
end

sol_true = solve(ODEProblem(sir!, [0.99, 0.01], (0.0, 40.0), (; β=β_true)),
                 Tsit5(); saveat=0.5)
t_data = collect(sol_true.t)
data_S = [sol_true.u[i][1] + 0.005 * randn() for i in 1:length(t_data)]
data_I = [sol_true.u[i][2] + 0.005 * randn() for i in 1:length(t_data)]
data_matrix = hcat(max.(data_S, 0.001), max.(data_I, 0.001))
```

## Fit: TwoStage vs BNG vs LAML

```{julia}
uf = BSplineApproximator(:β, (0.0, 0.5), 10)

prob = PSMProblem(sir!, [0.99, 0.01], (0.0, 40.0), [uf];
    data_times=t_data, data_values=Float64.(data_matrix),
    obs_to_state=[1, 2], known_params=NamedTuple())

sol_ts = solve(prob, TwoStageSolver(maxiters=1000, lr=0.01, verbose=false))
sol_bng = solve(prob, BNGSolver(maxiters=1000, lr=0.01, verbose=false))
sol_laml = solve(prob, LAML(maxiters=50, verbose=false))
```

### Recovered Force of Infection

```{julia}
I_grid = range(0.0, 0.5, length=100)
β_ts = sol_ts.unknown_functions[:β]
β_bng = sol_bng.unknown_functions[:β]
β_laml = sol_laml.unknown_functions[:β]

p1 = plot(I_grid, β_true.(I_grid), label="True β(I)", lw=2, color=:black,
          xlabel="I (infected fraction)", ylabel="β(I)",
          title="Force of Infection Recovery")
plot!(p1, I_grid, [β_ts(x) for x in I_grid], label="TwoStage", lw=2)
plot!(p1, I_grid, [β_bng(x) for x in I_grid], label="BNG", lw=2, ls=:dash)
plot!(p1, I_grid, [β_laml(x) for x in I_grid], label="LAML", lw=2, ls=:dot)
p1
```

### Trajectory Comparison

```{julia}
p2 = plot(t_data, data_matrix[:, 2], seriestype=:scatter, label="Data (I)",
          xlabel="Time", ylabel="Infected", title="Epidemic Curve", ms=3, alpha=0.5)
plot!(p2, t_data, sol_ts.fitted_values[:, 2], label="TwoStage", lw=2)
plot!(p2, t_data, sol_laml.fitted_values[:, 2], label="LAML", lw=2, ls=:dash)
p2
```

### Summary

```{julia}
println("TwoStage: loss=$(round(sol_ts.data_loss, digits=4)), β(0.2)=$(round(β_ts(0.2), digits=4))")
println("BNG:      loss=$(round(sol_bng.data_loss, digits=4)), β(0.2)=$(round(β_bng(0.2), digits=4))")
println("LAML:     loss=$(round(sol_laml.data_loss, digits=4)), β(0.2)=$(round(β_laml(0.2), digits=4))")
println("True:     β(0.2)=$(round(β_true(0.2), digits=4))")
```

## When to Use TwoStage

- **Fastest** solver — no ODE integration, simple spline smoothing + Adam
- Good for **initial exploration** before switching to more sophisticated methods
- Works well with **dense, low-noise** data where spline derivatives are accurate
- Less accurate than LAML when data is sparse or noisy (smoothing artifacts propagate)
